content.full_text:
Samuel L. Williams 
Los Angeles County Bar Association 
606 South Olive Street, Suite 1212 
Los Angeles, California 90014 
Dear Mr. Williams: 
May 12, 1978 
Thank you for your letter dated ~~ch 28, 1978 requesting an 
opinion from the Fair Political Practices Commission ("Commission R ) 
regarding the lobbying provisions of the Political Reform Act 
("Act"). 
Since your letter raises no substantive questions involv-
ing an interpretation of the Act, no formal opinion will be issued. 
I hope the following informal advice will be helpful. 
Your letter requests advice regarding whether certain expenses 
incurred by the Los Angeles County Bar Association's ("Association") 
Barristers Committee on Legislation ("Committee"), a committee 
recently formed to monitor and respond to legislative developments 
of interest to the Association, are payments to influence legisla-
tive or administrative action which may require the Association to 
file a disclosure report pursuant to Government Code Section 
86108(b).!/ Your letter poses a number of specific questions to 
which I will respond in the order presented. 
First, you have inquired whether the Association's reimburse-
ment of travel, food and lodging expenses of its members, relative 
to their activities for the Committee, are payments to influence 
legislative or administrative action. 
"Payment to influence 
legislative or administrative action" is defined in Section 82045 
to include the following specific types of payments: 
(a) 
Direct or indirect payment to a lobbyist ••• 
(b) 
Payment in support or assistance of a lobbyist ... 
(c) 
Payment which directly or indfrectly benefits any 
elective state official, legislative official or 
agency official ••• 
1/ All further statutory references are to the·Government Code. 


Samuel L. Williams 
-2-
May 12, 1978 
Cd} 
(e) 
Payment ••• for or in connection with direct communi-
cation with any elective state official, legislative 
official or agency official ••• 
Payment for or in connection with soliciting or urging 
other persons to enter into direft communication with 
any elective state official, legislative official or 
agency official. 
The Commission has adopted a regulation, 2 Cal. Admin. Code 
Section 18621, a copy of which is enclosed, that clarifies what 
payments to influence legislative or administrative action must be 
combined to determine if a report must be filed pursuant to Section 
86l08(b).~ Subsection ( ) (5) of that regulation provides that the 
following payments, except for compensation of employees and over-
head, must be included in that determination: 
Any payment or portion thereof which would not have been 
incurred but for the direct communication, or the solicit-
ing or urging of others to communicate directly with any 
elective state official, legislative official or agency 
official for the purpose of influencing legislative or 
administrative action. 
Applying that regulation to the Association's reimbursement of 
member's food and lodging expenses, if the members would have incur-
red those expenses whether or not direct communication with officials 
or soliciting or urging others to communicate with official took 
place, then the reimbursements do not have to be considered for pur-
poses of Section 86l08(b). 
For example, if the Association reimbursed 
a member for expenses incurred for traveling to Sacramento to attend 
a legislative hearing, as part of the regular ongoing function of 
monitoring legislative bills, then the expenses would not have to be 
included for purposes of Section 86l08(b) even if after the member 
returned home, the member entered into direct communication with 
state officiabas a result of the decision reached at the hearing. 
Secondly, you have inquired whether expenses, including overhead 
and salaries, incurred relative to communication of Committee activi-
ties to the Association's members through magazines, newsletters, or 
other mailings are payments to influence legislative or administra-
tive action. 
As described above, payments to influence legislatiVe 
or a~~inistrative action include payments for soliciting or urging 
other persons to enter into direct comnunication with state officials 
(Section 82045{e». 
Accordingly, if any publication of the Association 
solicits or urges members to enter into direct communication with 
state officials, then the expenses must be considered for purposes of 
2/ You might be interest to know that the Cbw~ission is proposing a 
legislative change in Section 86l08(b) to raise the monthly 
threshold from $250 to $2500 (SB 2077). 


~amueL L. W1ll1ams 
-3-
May 12, 1978 
Section 86l08{b). 
Only that portion of the expenses relating to the 
soliciting or urging of others to communicate would be includable. 
Overhead and salaries are includable to the extent specified in 
2 Cal. Admin. Code Section l862l(a) (4). 
Communications which merely 
present a position on a certain matter and do not "solicit or urge" 
a direct communication are not reportable.~ 
For your information, the Commission staff has recommended in 
a proposed amendment to 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18621 that the 
normal costs of producing a regularly published newsletter or period-
ical whose circulation is limited to an organization's members, 
employees, shareholders and other interested persons should not be 
includable for purposes of determining if a disclosure report must 
be filed pursuant to Section 86108(b).3/ 
Thirdly, you have inquired whether expenses incurred by the 
Association relative to drafting model legislation and its sU~$quent 
introduction in the Legislature are payments to influence legislative 
or administrative actio~ "Legislative action" is defined in Section 
82037 as: 
••• the drafting, introduction, consideration, modification, 
enactment or defeat of any bill, resolution, amendment, report, nomi-
nation or other matter by the Legislature ••• 
It is the opinion of 
the Commission staff that internal discussions of possible legislation, 
drafting of proposed language and circulation of the proposed language 
for comment to the Association's members are not legislative actions, 
so long as there is no communication with legislative officials regard-
ing specific matters. 
However, any direct communication with officials 
regarding the introduction or drafting 
proposed legislation, whether 
or not it is ever introduced, will be considered influencing legisla-
tive action. 
Therefore, any expenses incurred by the Association in 
connection with or subsequent to the initial direct communication on 
a particular matter are payments to influence legislative action and 
must be considered, to the extent specified in 2 Cal. Admin. Code 
Section 18621, for the purposes of determining if a reporting obliga-
tion arises pursuant to Section 96l08(b). 
Your final question concerns whether the reimbursement by a 
member's direct employer for food, lodging and travel expenses incur-
red relative to Committee activities will be reportable as payments 
to influence legislative or administrative action. 
Section 82045, 
which defines payment to influence legislative or administrative 
action, makes no distinction regarding who actually pays an expense. 
Therefore, a member's direct employer must consider the payments of 
reimbursed expenses, to the extent specified in 2 Cal. Admin. Code 
Section 18621, in determining if the direct employer might incur a 
l! A copy of this draft regulation as it was considered by the 
Commission at its May, 1978 meeting, is enclosed for your review. 
The proposed amendment is scheduled for adoption at the Commission's 
meeting in August. 
The staff would welcome any comments you have. 


:.:;amue.L L. \'vl.L.LlamS 
-4-
May 12, 1978 
reporting obligation pursuant to Section 86l08(b). 
In addition, if 
a member is compensated by his or her direct employer for the time 
the member devotes to Committee activites, the member's compensation 
may be a payment to influence legislative or administrative action 
if it is paid for any of the activities sp~cified in Section 82045. 
(See 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section l862l(a) (1, (3) and (4)) 
Incidentally, such compensation is considered to be "economic 
consideration" for the purpose of determining whether an individual 
is a lobbyist (Section 82039). 
Of course, the time tests specified 
in 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section l8239(e) would also have to be met 
before an individual qualified as a lobbyist. 
I hope this information is helpful. 
If you have any additional 
questions, please contact me. 
Sincerely, 
Alan Herndon 
Compliance Representative

Por your information, the Commission staff has recommended in

In addition, if 
a member is compensated by his or her direct employer for the time 
the member devotes to Committee activites, the member's compensation 
may 
a payment to influence legislative or administrative action 
if it is paid for any of the activities specified in Section 82045. 
(See 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18621(a) (1) (3) and (4» 
Incidentally, such compensation is considered to be "economic 
consideration" for the purpose of determining whether an individual 
is a lobbyist (Section 82039). 
Of course, the time tests specified 
in 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18239(e) would also have to be met 
before an individual qualified as a lobbyist. 
I hope this information is helpful. If you have any additional 
questions, please contact me. 
Sincerely, 
Alan Herndon 
Compliance Representative 
AH:bw 
Enclosure 


los Angeles 
Bar Association 
March 28, 1978 
Samuel L. 
President 
Mr. Daniel Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
110 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95815 
Dear Mr. Lowenstein: 
Suite 1212 
606 South Olive Street 
Aos Angeles, California 90014 
U13 620-6200 
Pursuant to authority found at Section 34114(a) of the 
California Political Reform Act of 1974 (the "Act") 
(Gov't Code, 
~g8l000 et seg.), the Los Angeles County 
Bar Association (the"Association") respectfully requests 
an advisory opinion in response to the matters set forth 
below. 
Since guidance relative to these matters is of 
some urgency, the Association requests the written advice 
of the Executive Director pursuant to Section 83114(b) of 
the Act as interpreted at Section 18329, Title 2, Division 
6 of the California Administrative Code (the "Code fl
) pend-
ing issuance of a formal advisory opinion or, alternatively, 
if the Commission determines the Association's request as 
not meriting issuance of an advisory opinion. 
The Los Angeles County Bar Association is a non-profit cor-
poration whose membership consists of the voluntary asso-
ciation of attorneys residing or practicing law within Los 
Angeles County. 
Recently, the Barristers Section of the Association (com-
posed of Association members who are thirty-five years old 
or younger) formed the Barrister's Committee on Legislation 
(the "Committee"). 
The purposes for which the Committee was 
formed primarily include monitoring and responding to legis-
lative developments in California of interest to the Asso-
ciation and its membership, as well as possible drafting and 
procuring introduction of its own legislative proposals. 
The 
Association neither employes nor contracts for the services 
of a "lobbyist" as that term is defined in Section 82039 of 
the Act and Section 18239 of the Code. 
Members of the Asso-
ciation who will be participating, directly or indirectly, 
Trustees 
Samuel L. Williams 
John D. Taylor 
John H. Brinsley 
Joseph D. Mandel 
Roy H. Aaron 
Jarrett S. Anderson 
Benjamin Aranda, III 
M. John Carson 
George W. Echan, Jr. 
Max A. Goodman 
Laurie Harris 
Alex Hoften 
David A. Horowitz 
Joseph G. Hurley 
Lionel B. LeBel, Jr. 
Charles T. Manatt 
Rose Matsui Ochi 
Mary S. Parker 
Martha S. Robinson 
Bert Z. Tigerman 
William W. Vaughn 
Robert S. Warren 
Harold W. Wax 
Earl P. Willens 
William A. Williams 


Mr. Daniel Lowenstein 
March 28, 1978 
Page two 
in Committee activities will do so without compensation of 
any kind and are not "employees" of the Association in any 
manner. 
However, certain employees of the Association will, 
from time to time, lend support, directly or indirectly, to 
the Committee's activities. 
For purposes of determining whether the Association makes 
"payments to influence legislative or administrative action" 
within the meaning of Section 82045 of the Act, the following 
specific inquiries, the subject of this advisory opinion re-
quest, are presented to the Commission: 
1. 
If the Association reimburses expenses of its members 
incurred for travel, lodging, food, etc. relative to 
Committee activities generally has it made a "payment 
to influence" within the meaning of 1382045(e) of the Act? 
(a) 
If these expenses were incurred relative to specific 
legislation of interest to the Association and/or the 
Committee? 
(b) 
If the expenses are incurred for a Committee member's 
efforts to monitor legislation of interest (without 
any "direct communication"), and report back to the 
Committee on the status of same, knowing that the re-
port will ultimately result in a Committee publication 
to all Association members which, in turn, may trigger 
spontaneous "influencing" of such legislation by various 
Association members? 
Does the answer to this subquestion 
(b) differ if the Association generally solicits the 
spontaneous response of its membership to the Committee's 
legislation status publication? 
2. 
Are Association expenses incurred relative to publication 
and support of Committee activities generally, (mailing 
meeting agendas, publication of a monthly magazine, etc.) 
"payments to influence" within the meaning of 1382045 of the 
Act? 
(a) 
Does it matter if any supported Committee publications 
specifically solicit or urge direct communication with 
members of the legislature concerning specific legis-
lation? 
(b) 
Does it matter if the "charter" given the Committee 
by the Association generally solicits or urges direct 
communication with members of the legislature regarding 
~ 
Committee publication? 


Mr. Daniel Lowenstein 
March 28, 1978 
Page three 
(c) 
Does it matter if the publications refer to 
specific legislation, convey an Association 
"position" thereto, but do not specifically 
urge or solicit "direct communication"? 
(d) 
Are expenses incurred for overhead, operating 
costs, salaries, etc. relative to Association 
employees' support of the Committee activities 
includable (to the extent specified in 818621 
of the Code) as a "payment to influence"? 
3. 
If a Committee member or members draft model legislation 
and are later successful in having it introduced in the 
legislature, are Association expenses incurred relative 
to its staff support of publication of various drafts of 
the model legislation for comment, or incurred relative 
to Association and/or Committee meetings to consider the 
model legislative language, etc. "payments to influence"? 
4. 
If an Association member incurs expenses of the nature 
described in question number 1, above, which are not reim-
bursed by the Association but rather by the member's regular 
employer (corporation, firm, client, etc.), does such mem-
ber's employer make a "payment to influence"? 
Even if the 
particular legislation in question is of no interest what-
ever to the employer? 
(a) 
If such a member is permitted by his/her employer to 
engage in Committee "influencing" activities during 
normal working hours (thus entitling the member to 
receive income) does such a person receive "economic 
consideration" within the meaning of the definition 
of "lobbyist" found at S82039 of the Act? 
Could such 
an e'mployer become an "empJpoyer of lobbyists" within 
the meaning of S86108 of the Act by virtue of such 
employee's activities (provided the employee otherwise 
meets the definitional requirements of a "lobbyist")? 
(b) 
Is a portion of a member's salary who engages in 
activities described in subquestion (a), above, a 
"payment to influence"? 
rdially, 
SAMUEL L. WILLIAMS

sections.question: s, please contact me.
sections.conclusion: The Commission provides informal advice that: (1) member reimbursements for food and lodging need not be reported if the expenses would have been incurred regardless of direct communication with officials; (2) publication expenses relating to soliciting or urging direct communication with officials must be reported, but mere position statements are not reportable; (3) internal drafting and circulation of proposed legislation are not reportable, but any direct communication with officials regarding legislation triggers reporting obligations; (4) a member's direct employer must consider reimbursed expenses and compensation for Committee time in determining reporting obligations, and such compensation may constitute economic consideration for determining lobbyist status.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether various expenses incurred by a bar association's legislative committee—including member reimbursements for travel and meals, publication costs, and legislative drafting—constitute reportable payments to influence legislative or administrative action under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that expenses are reportable as payments to influence legislative action only if they would not have been incurred but for direct communication with officials or solicitation to such officials; mere position statements and internal legislative drafting are not reportable, but any direct official contact triggers reporting requirements; employers reimbursing employees for legislative committee activities must consider these payments for disclosure purposes.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR-scanned document with some text degradation but core content clearly readable. No formal opinion issued per FPPC determination that letter raised no substantive interpretive questions. Document includes substantive regulatory guidance on Section 82045 payment definitions and application of 2 Cal. Admin. Code Section 18621.

CONCLUSION: The Commission provides informal advice that: (1) member reimbursements for food and lodging need not be reported if the expenses would have been incurred regardless of direct communication with officials; (2) publication expenses relating to soliciting or urging direct communication with officials must be reported, but mere position statements are not reportable; (3) internal drafting and circulation of proposed legislation are not reportable, but any direct communication with officials regarding legislation triggers reporting obligations; (4) a member's direct employer must consider reimbursed expenses and compensation for Committee time in determining reporting obligations, and such compensation may constitute economic consideration for determining lobbyist status.

embedding.summary: In response to the Los Angeles County Bar Association's inquiry, the FPPC provides informal advice on which expenses of a legislative monitoring committee constitute reportable payments to influence legislative or administrative action under the Political Reform Act and Government Code Section 86108(b).
